food_subgroup: Whole oats, steel-cut, cooked; minimally processed oat groats cut into pieces by steel blades, boiled in water

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steel-cut oats cooked; general oat food concepts exist but no exact match confirmed.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 cup of cooked steel-cut oats (approximately 234 g), prepared by boiling in water with no additions.

nutrient_source.title: Oats, cooked with water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173904)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values correspond to cooked oatmeal (steel-cut / whole oat groats, water-cooked, no salt) from USDA SR Legacy. Per-100g values verified against input record and cross-checked with USDA FoodData Central cooked oats entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Steel-Cut / Porridge Oats (boiled) — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 42 (low) sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for steel-cut / porridge oats boiled in water. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (42 × 28.1g) / 100 ≈ 11.8. Available carbohydrate per 234g serving = 12.0 g/100g × 2.34 = 28.1g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium; beta-glucan soluble fibre supports LDL-C reduction and cardiovascular metabolic health
hypertension.guidance: Steel-cut oats are suitable for hypertensive patients: sodium is only ~9 mg per 234 g serving, and 3 g of beta-glucan per cup meets the EFSA-approved daily dose for LDL-C reduction of 5–10%, while potassium (143 mg) and magnesium (61 mg, ~15% RDA) provide additional mild blood-pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (42) and low GL (11.8 per cup); beta-glucan delays gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steel-cut oats (GI 42, GL 11.8 per cup) are strongly preferred over instant oats (GI ~65) for Type 2 Diabetes management: the viscous beta-glucan gel delays gastric emptying and glucose absorption, and a documented second-meal effect further reduces postprandial glucose at the following meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beta-glucan (3 g per cup) with EFSA-approved LDL-C reduction claim; very low saturated fat (0.7 g per cup)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steel-cut oats provide 3 g of beta-glucan per cup, meeting the EFSA-approved threshold for a clinically meaningful LDL-C reduction of 5–10%; combined with very low saturated fat (0.7 g per cup), negligible trans fat, and no cholesterol, they represent a cardioprotective whole-grain choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan immune modulation via Dectin-1 receptors; magnesium anti-inflammatory properties; high-fibre gut microbiome support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steel-cut oats are suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients: beta-glucan modulates innate immune response via Dectin-1 receptors, magnesium (61 mg per cup, ~15% RDA) has documented anti-inflammatory properties, and the high soluble fibre content supports a gut microbiome environment associated with reduced systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steel-cut oats (also called Irish oats, Scottish oats, or pinhead oats) are whole oat groats that have been cut into two or three pieces by steel blades, retaining the bran, germ, and endosperm intact. When cooked by boiling in water for 20–30 minutes (or after overnight soaking, 5–10 minutes), they produce a thick, chewy porridge with a nutty flavour and a substantially denser texture than rolled or instant oats. One standard serving is 1 cup cooked (234 g), providing approximately 166 kcal, 5.9 g protein, 3.5 g fat, 28.1 g carbohydrate, and 4.0 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is 42 (low), compared to instant oats at GI ~65 and white rice at GI ~72 — a clinically critical distinction for Type 2 Diabetes management. The 3 g of beta-glucan per cup meets the EFSA-approved daily dose for LDL-C reduction of 5–10%. Beta-glucan forms a viscous gel in the gastrointestinal tract, delaying gastric emptying and attenuating postprandial glucose and insulin responses; a documented second-meal effect further reduces postprandial glucose at the subsequent meal. Sodium is negligible (~9 mg per cup), making steel-cut oats appropriate for hypertensive patients. Magnesium (61 mg per cup, ~15% RDA) and potassium (143 mg per cup) provide additional cardiovascular benefit. Steel-cut oats are vegan, dairy-free, and halal, and are widely available in Singapore at Cold Storage and major supermarkets under brands such as Bob's Red Mill, John McCann's, and Quaker Steel Cut Oats.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy for cooked oats (exact match). GI value of 42 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for steel-cut / porridge oats boiled in water (exact match). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI data are fully supported by approved primary sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked steel-cut oats is not separately reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry used; the total fat figure (1.5 g/100g) is confirmed. water_g set to -1: moisture content for cooked steel-cut oats not separately extracted from the source entry used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked oats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for plain cooked oats). No zero-macro justification required: protein_g (2.5), fat_g (1.5), and carbohydrate_g (12.0) are all non-zero. GI source note: University of Sydney GI Database lists steel-cut / porridge oats boiled in water at GI ~42; this is a direct match. GL per serving calculated as (42 × 28.08 g available carbohydrate per 234 g serving) / 100 = 11.8. This food is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Western/health-food cuisine category requires English, Chinese, and Malay coverage, which are all present. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid and moisture data for cooked steel-cut oats are not available in the USDA SR Legacy entry (FDC ID 173904) used as the primary nutrient source.
